summary:
This dental chart adheres to the Universal Numbering System as it assigns sequential numerical identifiers across both upper and lower jaws without using quadrant-specific symbols found in other systems like Palmer Notation.

The summary accurately captures the essence by highlighting the use of continuous numbering typical of the Universal Numbering System while contrasting this approach against alternative methods such as those used under Palmer Notation. \]
To summarize:

- The given dental chart matches the pattern of assigning single-digit or double-digit numerals sequentially throughout all four jaw sections, aligning closely with the principles of the Universal Numbering System.
- It does not employ the symbol-and-quadrant method seen in Palmer Notation but instead opts for straightforward numeric identification consistent with UN standards. \( \)
 Agent2 concludes that the depicted system corresponds to the Universal Numbering System due to its uniform application of numbered labels irrespective of whether one considers right versus left sides or top versus bottom halves of the mouth. To condense further into two sentences focusing solely on critical aspects related directly back towards answering what specific standard was followed here,

This representation utilizes straight numeral assignments spanning entire arches uniformly—characteristic only of the Universal Numbering System—and diverges significantly enough visually compared to more complex schemes involving distinct symbols per side/quadrent utilized elsewhere; hence confirming compliance strictly within U.S.'s standardized notation protocol. $ \$$
 
Therefore,
